summary_ja: 英国では毎週数百から数千件規模で、犯罪組織が脆弱な人の自宅を不法に占拠し薬物の保管・販売拠点にする「クックーイング」が起きていると警察が警告した。ロンドンでは被害報告が急増しており、被害者は自宅の囚人のように扱われる事例も多い。クックーイングは年内に独立した犯罪となり最高5年の刑が科される見込みだが、警察の対応や被害者支援には課題が残る。

sentences.text: Hundreds, if not thousands, of people's homes are being taken over by criminals every week in the UK, usually to store and deal drugs, police chiefs have told the BBC.
sentences.grammar_ja: 「if not thousands」は「数千に及ぶかもしれない」という推定の挿入表現。後半の police chiefs have told the BBC は報道文の倒置（主語＋動詞が文末）。

sentences.text: Perpetrators of so-called cuckooing often target vulnerable people, including the elderly or disabled, by forcing themselves into homes to carry out illegal acts.
sentences.grammar_ja: so-called＋名詞で「いわゆる〜」。by + -ing で手段を示す。including は具体例の列挙。

sentences.text: In some cases drug users are exploited by gangs who move in and refuse to leave.
sentences.grammar_ja: 受動態 are exploited。関係代名詞 who は gangs を修飾し、move in and refuse to leave と並列。

sentences.text: Cuckooing is not yet a specific criminal offence so there is limited data on how widespread it is.
sentences.grammar_ja: not yet で「まだ〜ではない」。so で結果を示す。on how widespread it is は data を修飾。

sentences.text: However, figures shared exclusively with the BBC show 1,539 incidents of cuckooing were reported to police in London between May 2025 and April 2026.
sentences.grammar_ja: 過去分詞 shared exclusively with... が figures を修飾。show の後は that 省略の内容節。

sentences.text: Of those, 1,275 of the victims were male.
sentences.grammar_ja: Of those で前文の数字のうちから一部を指す。ニュースで頻出の内訳表現。

sentences.text: The National Police Chiefs' Council (NPCC) said "horrific things" have happened to victims, who were often trapped in their own homes.
sentences.grammar_ja: 現在完了 have happened で過去から現在への影響。非制限用法の who 節で追加情報を付加。

sentences.text: "We've had cases where they've been forced to eat dog excrement or perform sexual acts, and those will be recorded and then used as a form of blackmail for the perpetrator to say 'if you don't do as I say, then ultimately we'll share this with friends and we'll put this on social media'," the NPCC's Kirsten Dent, told the BBC.
sentences.grammar_ja: cases where... で事例内容を説明。be forced to + 動詞原形。as a form of blackmail で「脅迫の手段として」。

sentences.text: "It's hidden and in people's homes, it's not always easy to detect."
sentences.grammar_ja: 形式主語 it + be + adj + to detect の構文。and で2つの理由を並列。

sentences.text: Cuckooing - named after cuckoos, who frequently take over other birds' nests to lay their own eggs - is expected to become a specific criminal offence by the end of the year, carrying a maximum five-year prison sentence.
sentences.grammar_ja: ダッシュ内の過去分詞 named after... は挿入説明。is expected to become で予定・見込み。carrying... は付帯状況の分詞構文。

sentences.text: It forms part of the Crime and Policing Act 2026, but the government is yet to issue statutory guidance to police forces before the law can be enforced.
sentences.grammar_ja: be yet to + 動詞原形で「まだ〜していない」。before the law can be enforced は時を表す副詞節。

sentences.text: Ahead of the implementation of the law change, the BBC accompanied London's Metropolitan Police as officers visited suspected cuckooing properties and witnessed shocking signs of squalor.
sentences.grammar_ja: Ahead of で「〜に先立って」。as は「〜している間に」の副詞的用法。

sentences.text: We also spoke to victims who said they felt like prisoners in their own homes and feared going to the police in case of violent reprisals.
sentences.grammar_ja: feel like + 名詞。fear + -ing。in case of で「〜の場合に備えて／〜を恐れて」。

sentences.text: Jamie, 34, has brain damage after being struck over the head with a glass bottle and, as a result, he struggles to move and speak properly.
sentences.grammar_ja: after being struck は受動の動名詞。as a result で因果。struggle to + 動詞原形。

sentences.text: Two years ago, a criminal gang took advantage of his condition by befriending him, before moving into his home without his consent and using his flat as somewhere to sell drugs from.
sentences.grammar_ja: take advantage of で「〜を利用する」。before + -ing で時系列。somewhere to sell drugs from は不定詞の形容詞用法。

sentences.text: "People went from being really nice and sound to me, to just taking whatever they can," he said.
sentences.grammar_ja: go from A to B で変化を表す。whatever they can は「取れるものは何でも」。

sentences.text: "They robbed my clothes," he added.
sentences.grammar_ja: 直接話法＋he added の報道引用。rob は奪う（盗む）。

sentences.text: "They started taking everything worth taking at my house, like stealing it without you knowing.
sentences.grammar_ja: worth + -ing で「〜する価値がある」。without you knowing は without + 所有格 + -ing。

sentences.text: And when I did realise it was them, they would deny it."
sentences.grammar_ja: did realise は強調・対比の do。would deny で当時の習慣的な行動。

sentences.text: He said one of the most demeaning things was being slapped by a teenager who was much younger than him.
sentences.grammar_ja: one of the most + 形容詞 + 名詞。動名詞 being slapped は受動の意味。

sentences.text: Jamie said he felt it was impossible to be assertive with the gang because of his condition.
sentences.grammar_ja: it was impossible to... の形式主語構文。be assertive with で「〜に対して主張する」。

sentences.text: "I [have] got brain damage and one decent punch could do a lot of damage.
sentences.grammar_ja: could で可能性・仮定。[have] は編集上の補足。do damage はコロケーション。

sentences.text: I can't fight, I can't argue," he said.
sentences.grammar_ja: 並列の否定文。口語的な短い引用。

sentences.text: Earlier this year, he said he managed to escape from his flat and move to a different part of the country - fleeing the gang.
sentences.grammar_ja: manage to + 動詞原形で「何とか〜できた」。fleeing... は付帯状況の分詞。

sentences.text: The NPCC visited 683 suspected cuckooed addresses in early March this year as part of a week focusing on county lines - the act of moving drugs from big cities into smaller, rural areas.
sentences.grammar_ja: suspected cuckooed addresses は過去分詞の連鎖修飾。the act of -ing で名詞化した定義。

sentences.text: According to officers, county lines operations and cuckooing are intrinsically linked.
sentences.grammar_ja: According to で情報源を示す。be intrinsically linked で「本質的に結びついている」。

sentences.text: Dealers moving drugs often look for properties to operate from.
sentences.grammar_ja: 現在分詞 moving drugs が dealers を修飾。operate from は前置詞を文末に置く用法。

sentences.text: "We suspect hundreds - if not thousands - of properties are being cuckooed across the country (UK) every week," said Dent.
sentences.grammar_ja: be being + 過去分詞 の進行受動態。引用文の後に said Dent と主語・動詞を倒置。

sentences.text: One of those was Jackie, who told the BBC she used to be a heroin and cocaine addict.
sentences.grammar_ja: used to be で過去の状態。who 節で Jackie の追加情報。

sentences.text: Her dealer allowed her to run up a large drug debt.
sentences.grammar_ja: allow + 人 + to 不定詞。run up a debt で「借金を積み重ねる」。

sentences.text: When she could not pay it back, he added an extra £2,000 to the bill and said she had to have a dealer living with her until she could repay the money.
sentences.grammar_ja: had to have + 人 + -ing で「〜してもらわなければならなかった」。until 節で期限。

sentences.text: She said she was cuckooed for months, and became "a prisoner in my own home".
sentences.grammar_ja: 受動態 was cuckooed。become + 名詞で状態変化。直接引用は原文の一人称を保持。

sentences.text: "I asked him to leave so many times and he said: 'My guy said I've got to stay here'".
sentences.grammar_ja: so many times で頻度の強調。I've got to は have got to（〜しなければならない）の口語。

sentences.text: "It just meant that I had to stay in one room.
sentences.grammar_ja: mean that + 節で「つまり〜ということだった」。had to で義務・余儀なし。

sentences.text: I tried to get access to the front room and the kitchen and he just went, 'no, you're not coming in'.
sentences.grammar_ja: get access to で「〜に入る／使う権限を得る」。went, '...' は口語で「〜と言った」。

sentences.text: I had to stay in my bedroom.
sentences.grammar_ja: had to + 動詞原形で「〜せざるを得なかった」。短い叙述文。

sentences.text: Occasionally he might say 'I'll buy you chicken and chips or something' but it was very rare," she said.
sentences.grammar_ja: Occasionally で頻度。might say で過去のたまの行動。but で対比。

sentences.text: She said she used drugs to get through the ordeal.
sentences.grammar_ja: use ... to ... で目的。get through the ordeal は「苦難を乗り切る」。

sentences.text: After several months of occupying the property, the dealer finally left.
sentences.grammar_ja: After + 名詞/動名詞句 で時を示す。finally で遅れて起きた結果。

sentences.text: Jackie said suspicion of police watching him made him leave.
sentences.grammar_ja: made him leave は使役構文（make + 人 + 動詞原形）。suspicion of -ing で「〜ではないかという疑い」。

sentences.text: She is now free from drugs after going to rehab, and works with various support groups to raise awareness of cuckooing and how it can happen to anybody.
sentences.grammar_ja: free from で「〜から解放された」。raise awareness of で「〜への認知を高める」。

sentences.text: "It's not just about drugs.
sentences.grammar_ja: not just about で「〜だけではない」。短い引用の一部。

sentences.text: It can be about anything.
sentences.grammar_ja: can be about で可能性・多様性を示す。

sentences.text: It can be partners [not leaving].
sentences.grammar_ja: It can be + 名詞 で例示。[not leaving] は編集補足。

sentences.text: It can be family members.
sentences.grammar_ja: 並列する It can be... 構文で列挙を続ける。

sentences.text: It can be anybody that refuses to leave your property and it can be used for all sorts, money, stolen goods, anything they think they can exploit you", she said.
sentences.grammar_ja: that refuses... の関係節。be used for で用途。they think they can exploit you は目的語 you の省略に注意。

sentences.text: When the BBC visited the London properties with the Met police, officers said  squalid conditions were the norm when it comes to cuckooed premises.
sentences.grammar_ja: were the norm で「普通だった」。when it comes to で「〜に関して言えば」。

sentences.text: Packets of food were strewn across the floor, doors hung off their hinges, and a pungent smell of faeces filled the air.
sentences.grammar_ja: 3つの文が and で並列。were strewn / hung / filled の過去形。

sentences.text: We were taken to a kitchen with raw chicken dumped in the sink.
sentences.grammar_ja: 受動態 were taken。with + 名詞 + 過去分詞 で付帯状況。

sentences.text: There were blood stains on a duvet, and a filthy folding table was used as a bed.
sentences.grammar_ja: There were... の存在構文。be used as で「〜として使われた」。

sentences.text: A toilet in one bathroom was clogged.
sentences.grammar_ja: 受動態 was clogged（詰まっていた）。簡潔な状態描写。

sentences.text: All the properties contained a putrid stench.
sentences.grammar_ja: contain は「含む・漂わせる」。putrid は強い形容詞。

sentences.text: Officers from the Met - Britain's largest force - said they were seeing a pattern when it comes to those taken advantage of.
sentences.grammar_ja: those taken advantage of は「利用された人々」（take advantage of の受動）。

sentences.text: "What the data is showing us is that if you're a white male between 40 and 49 and potentially drug-dependent, then you're highly likely to become a victim of cuckooing.
sentences.grammar_ja: What... is that... の名詞節主語。highly likely to become で「〜になりやすい」。

sentences.text: "We're also seeing repeat victims where sadly people who are once cuckooed moved to a different location or remain at the same location become victims again," said Insp Andrew Cameron.
sentences.grammar_ja: 関係副詞 where の先行詞は victims。who are once cuckooed は受動の関係節。

sentences.text: Freedom of information figures obtained by the BBC show there were 380 investigations where cuckooing was flagged as a potential concern in the capital in the year 2023/24.
sentences.grammar_ja: obtained by the BBC が figures を修飾。was flagged as で「〜としてフラグが立てられた」。

sentences.text: This figure rose to 1,078 in 2025/26.
sentences.grammar_ja: rise to で数値への増加。簡潔な統計報道文。

sentences.text: The Met said the rise was primarily down to more awareness in the force about what constitutes cuckooing, as well as additional drug dealers using the tactic as a business model.
sentences.grammar_ja: be down to で「〜が原因だ」。what constitutes... は「何が〜に当たるか」の名詞節。

sentences.text: The force said the methodology of collecting the data had also changed during this time, which also could account for some of the increase.
sentences.grammar_ja: 過去完了 had changed。関係代名詞 which は前文全体を受ける。account for で「説明する」。

sentences.text: Until cuckooing becomes a specific offence, police officers said they were attempting to arrest suspects for other offences, such as drug possession or slavery offences.
sentences.grammar_ja: Until + 現在形 で「〜するまで」。attempt to arrest。such as で例示。

sentences.text: Sources across several police forces in England said closure orders were being used more widely to stop cuckooing.
sentences.grammar_ja: 過去進行受動 were being used。more widely で適用範囲の拡大。

sentences.text: They enable police officers and local authorities to temporarily close down a property.
sentences.grammar_ja: enable + 人 + to 不定詞。temporarily close down で一時閉鎖。

sentences.text: A partial closure order can mean the tenant is able to stay, but named visitors and other individuals are barred.
sentences.grammar_ja: mean (that)... の内容節。be barred で「立ち入り禁止」。

sentences.text: But some victims are critical of the way it is dealt with.
sentences.grammar_ja: be critical of で「〜を批判的に見る」。the way it is dealt with は関係副詞 the way + 節。

sentences.text: "I have no faith in the police.
sentences.grammar_ja: have no faith in で「〜を信頼しない」。固定コロケーション。

sentences.text: They're useless," said one victim we spoke to on condition of anonymity.
sentences.grammar_ja: on condition of anonymity で「匿名を条件に」。we spoke to は関係代名詞省略。

sentences.text: They believe vulnerable people need support and that more should be done to stop them being exploited.
sentences.grammar_ja: that 節の並列。stop + 人 + being exploited（動名詞の受動）。

sentences.text: The NPCC said it was working to build confidence among victims and believed the law change to make cuckooing a specific criminal offence would help.
sentences.grammar_ja: 過去進行 was working to...。to make... は目的の不定詞。would help で未来の効果。

sentences.text: Dr Amy Loughery, a University of Leeds researcher, whose work focuses on cuckooing and county lines exploitation, said police did not always recognise when vulnerable people were being exploited.
sentences.grammar_ja: 同格語 a University... researcher。非制限用法 whose。when 節は時・状況。

sentences.text: "Victims of cuckooing were not necessarily being seen as victims because often they had previous experiences or contact with the police," she said.
sentences.grammar_ja: not necessarily で部分否定。be seen as で「〜と見なされる」。because で理由。

sentences.text: While awareness of the issue has improved in recent years, she said "there is a significant way to go" and called for more guidance and policies to help identify exploitation and improve responses to cuckooing.
sentences.grammar_ja: While で譲歩「〜だが」。there is a way to go は慣用句。called for で「〜を求めた」。

sentences.text: A Home Office spokesperson said the testimonies given to BBC News were "appalling".
sentences.grammar_ja: 過去分詞 given to BBC News が testimonies を修飾。spokesperson は公式声明の主語。

sentences.text: They added: "We will do everything in our power to protect communities from the vile crime of cuckooing, including investing more than £34m this year in the County Lines Programme to target drug dealing gangs and organised crime groups."
sentences.grammar_ja: everything in our power to...。including + -ing で具体的内容。target は「標的にする」。

sentences.text: If you have been affected by any of the issues raised in this story, information and support can be found at the BBC's Action Line.
sentences.grammar_ja: If you have been affected by... の現在完了受動。issues raised in... は過去分詞修飾。

sentences.text: Daniel Day denies intentionally or recklessly causing a public nuisance by climbing the Elizabeth Tower.
sentences.grammar_ja: deny + -ing。intentionally or recklessly は副詞の並列。関連記事の見出し。

sentences.text: The musician whose real name was Mark Orabiyi - was "a gem", rapper Wretch 32 said.
sentences.grammar_ja: whose で所有関係。ダッシュで挿入。倒置した引用構文。

sentences.text: Chief Constable Ivan Balhatchet says he cannot argue with findings of a report.
sentences.grammar_ja: cannot argue with で「〜に異論を唱えられない」。関連記事見出し。

sentences.text: How a student's murder led to protests, political debate and scrutiny of the police.
sentences.grammar_ja: How から始まる見出しで経緯を示す。led to + 名詞。

sentences.text: Commissioner Sarah Taylor says she quit because of a relative's life-changing diagnosis
sentences.grammar_ja: because of + 名詞で理由。life-changing は複合形容詞。関連記事見出し。

vocabulary.meaning_ja: （犯罪者が脆弱な人の家に居座り不法利用する）クックーイング
vocabulary.example: Cuckooing is expected to become a specific criminal offence by the end of the year.

vocabulary.meaning_ja: 大都市から地方へ薬物を供給する組織的ルート
vocabulary.example: County lines operations and cuckooing are intrinsically linked.

vocabulary.meaning_ja: 脆弱な、被害を受けやすい
vocabulary.example: Perpetrators often target vulnerable people, including the elderly or disabled.
Those will be recorded and then used as a form of blackmail.
They feared going to the police in case of violent reprisals.
Her dealer allowed her to run up a large drug debt.
Officers witnessed shocking signs of squalor at suspected properties.

vocabulary.meaning_ja: （警察などによる）住宅の一時閉鎖命令
vocabulary.example: Closure orders were being used more widely to stop cuckooing.

grammar_points.title: Approximation with 'if not'
grammar_points.rule: Use 'X, if not Y' to suggest the real number may be even higher than X.
grammar_points.example: Hundreds, if not thousands, of people's homes are being taken over every week.
grammar_points.meaning_ja: 「数百、いや数千に及ぶかもしれない」のように、前の数字を上回る可能性を示すニュース記事でよく使われる表現。

grammar_points.title: Passive voice in crime reporting
grammar_points.rule: Passive structures (be + past participle) focus on victims and actions rather than agents.
grammar_points.example: Drug users are exploited by gangs who move in and refuse to leave.
grammar_points.meaning_ja: 犯罪報道では受動態で被害者や事件の内容に焦点を当て、加害者は by 句や関係節で後から示すことが多い。

grammar_points.title: Reporting clauses with inversion
grammar_points.rule: After a long quote, the reporting verb and subject can follow: '...,' said Name.
grammar_points.example: "We suspect hundreds of properties are being cuckooed every week," said Dent.
grammar_points.meaning_ja: 長い直接引用のあとに said + 人名 を置く倒置は英語新聞の定型パターン。

grammar_points.title: Participle phrases for extra detail
grammar_points.rule: Past/present participles (-ed/-ing) add information about nouns without a full relative clause.
grammar_points.example: Cuckooing - named after cuckoos, who take over other birds' nests - is expected to become a criminal offence.
grammar_points.meaning_ja: 過去分詞・現在分詞句を名詞に付け加え、関係代名詞節を省略して情報を圧縮する書き方。
